Document [0] (Title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination Findings) PE is a well-recognized cause of sudden cardiac arrest (8%). [27] A massive PE leads to an acute right ventricular failure, which presents as jugular venous distension, parasternal lift, third heart sound, cyanosis, and shock. In a patient with PE who has tachycardia on presentation and develops sudden bradycardia or a new broad complex tachycardia (with right bundle branch block), clinicians should consider the right ventricular strain and impending shock. PE should be suspected in anyone with hypotension with jugular venous distension when acute myocardial infarction, pericardial tamponade, or tension pneumothorax has been ruled out. [28]

Document [4] (Title: Pleurisy -- History and Physical) Additional clues from the patient's history and physical examination can help narrow the differential diagnosis and guide diagnostic testing. Dyspnea raises concerns for pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, or pneumothorax. Tachycardia and tachypnea further increase suspicion of pulmonary embolism or pneumothorax. Cough, fever, and sputum production are more indicative of pneumonia. Pain radiating to the arm or neck may suggest a cardiac origin, particularly if accompanied by tachycardia, tachypnea, nausea, diaphoresis, vomiting, or palpitations. Pain that worsens when lying down but improves when leaning forward suggests pericarditis, particularly if accompanied by a pericardial friction rub. Unlike a pleural friction rub, a pericardial friction rub persists even during breath-holding.

Document [8] (Title: Thromboembolic Event -- History and Physical) In patients with established PE, tachypnea (respiratory rate over 18/min) is the most common physical findings. Document [9] (Title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism -- History and Physical -- Clinical History) A timely diagnosis of PE is crucial because of the high associated mortality and morbidity, which may be prevented with early treatment. Notably, 30% of untreated patients with PEs die, while only 8% die after timely therapy. [22] [23] Unfortunately, diagnosing PE can be difficult due to the variety of nonspecific clinical signs and symptoms in patients with acute PE. The most common symptoms of PE include dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, cough, hemoptysis, presyncope, and syncope. Dyspnea may be acute and severe in a central PE, whereas mild and transient dyspnea is observed in small peripheral PE. In patients with preexisting heart failure or pulmonary disease, worsening dyspnea may be the only symptom. Chest pain is a frequent symptom usually caused by pleural irritation due to distal emboli causing pulmonary infarction. [24] In central PE, chest pain may be from underlying right ventricular ischemia and needs to be differentiated from an acute coronary syndrome or aortic dissection.
Document [10] (Title: Dyspnea on Exertion(Archived) -- History and Physical) The physical exam should begin with a rapid assessment of the ABCs (airway, breathing, and circulation). Once determined to be stable, a full physical exam can be done. To determine the severity of dyspnea, one needs to observe respiratory effort, use of accessory muscles, mental status, and ability to speak. Engorgement of the neck veins may imply cor pulmonale caused by severe COPD, congestive heart failure, or cardiac tamponade. Thyromegaly may indicate hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism. Percussion of the lung lobes for dullness can determine the presence or absence of consolidation and effusion. Hyperresonance on percussion is a worrisome finding that indicates possible pneumothorax or severe bullous emphysema. Lung auscultation may reveal absent breath sounds indicating the presence of a region-occupying mass, such as pleural effusion or malignancy. The presence of wheezing is highly consistent with the diagnosis of obstructive lung diseases such as asthma or COPD. However, wheezing may be associated with pulmonary edema or pulmonary embolism. Pulmonary edema and pneumonia may present with rales on auscultation. Auscultation of the heart may reveal the presence of dysrhythmia, cardiac murmurs, or aberrant heart gallops. An S3 gallop indicates cardiac overfilling seen in left ventricular systolic dysfunction and congestive heart failure (CHF). An S4 gallop suggests left ventricular dysmotility and dysfunction. A loud P2 indicates possible pulmonary hypertension. Murmurs may indicate valvular dysfunction. Diminished heart sounds may indicate cardiac tamponade. Pericarditis may present with a rubbing cardiac sound on auscultation. On abdominal examination, hepatomegaly, ascites, and positive hepatojugular reflux may suggest a diagnosis of CHF. Lower extremity edema is associated with CHF, and extreme swelling of the extremities suggests possible deep venous thrombosis that can lead to a pulmonary embolism. Digital clubbing is present in some forms of lung malignancy or severe chronic hypoxia. Cyanosis of the extremities indicates hypoxia. [6]

Document [12] (Title: Pulmonary Infarction(Archived) -- History and Physical) Given pulmonary embolism serves as the etiology of pulmonary infarction in most cases, it is important to understand the clinical picture representing both conditions. The presenting features of a pulmonary infarction overlap those of a pulmonary embolism with a few important distinctions. In patients with concurrent pulmonary embolism and pulmonary infarction, the following features are present: dyspnea (69% to 78%), chest pain (49% to 70%), swelling or pain in a unilateral lower extremity (27% to 31%), fever (5% to 11%), and hemoptysis (4% to 19%). [1] [3] [12] Presenting features in all patients with the diagnosis of pulmonary embolism, regardless of the presence of pulmonary infarction, included dyspnea (72% to 75%), chest pain (36% to 38%), signs of DVT (22% to 33%), hemoptysis (4% to 8%). [1] [20] Thus, pleuritic chest pain and hemoptysis were both more common in patients with pulmonary embolism and pulmonary infarction versus those with pulmonary embolism and no pulmonary infarction. Symptoms noted to have no statistical difference between the 2 groups include cough, syncope, sudden onset dyspnea, signs of DVT, fever, and right ventricular overload. Among patients incidentally found to have a pulmonary infarction after a biopsy of a radiographically discovered lung nodule, 65% of patients had no respiratory complaints, 26% had dyspnea, 7% had chest pain, and 5% had hemoptysis. [4] This data highlights the difficulty of diagnosing a pulmonary infarction and emphasizes the importance of considering a broad differential diagnosis for patients who present with symptoms that could be secondary to a pulmonary infarction.

The mediastinum is a primary anatomic site for such diseases and is situated beneath the chest (hrudník), which itself is the overarching region for pleural conditions including pleurisy, pneumothorax and pulmonary collapse. Pleural disorders constitute the broader category for all pathologies of the pleural cavity (pleurální dutina); specific entities include neoplasms of uncertain behavior of the pleura, hemothorax, empyema and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right, left, bilateral). Diagnostic evaluation relies chiefly on computed tomography of the chest (CT scan chest), a

Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678) Abdominal paracentesis is a medical procedure that is directly associated with الاسْتِسْقَاء (ascites). It is performed to obtain peritoneal fluid for diagnostic evaluation or to therapeutically drain excess fluid in patients who have ascites, thereby assisting clinicians in determining the underlying cause of the fluid accumulation and providing symptom relief.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 249) The mediastinum is the central compartment of the thorax and a key anatomic subdivision within the chest, serving as the direct site for a variety of thoracic computed tomography (CT) examinations. Chest CT—including CT angiography of the thorax with contrast, non‑contrast diagnostic thoracic CT, and related protocols that add contrast—constitutes a primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal structures and is classified under the broader group of chest and respiratory‑tract imaging procedures. Chest CT functions as the principal finding method for multiple mediastinal abnormalities, such as a mass of the mid‑mediastinum, a mass of the posterior mediastinum, a mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, a mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. The first three entities are categorized as mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), while mediastinal effusion is classified as a mediastinal disorder (اضطراب منصفي). Chest CT also detects complications
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1213) Other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is classified as a subtype of اضطراب منصفي. The imaging finding described as computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is also identified as an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Consequently, both the clinical entity other lower respiratory disease and the radiologic observation of mediastinal effusion share the same broader disorder category, اضطراب منصفي, while the respiratory disease specifically carries a risk of developing an abscess.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2872) Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as vascular conditions, and the vascular category also encompasses primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). These entities represent distinct manifestations of abnormal pressure within the circulatory system, illustrating how various forms of hypertension and pulmonary vascular disease fall under the broader umbrella of vascular pathology. Recognizing and managing these vascular pressure abnormalities is essential for comprehensive patient care.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914) Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.
